_note: Curated Florida 2026 election cycle (demo data). Structured to be API-ready: candidate_ids point into candidates.json, office/level are stable classifications, and ballot_measures are grouped by state and county so a personalized ballot can be built from (state, county, congressional_district, state_senate_district, state_house_district, city_slug).
_source: Manually curated; replace with Ballotpedia / FL Division of Elections / county Supervisor of Elections feeds when available.
_closed_primary_note: Florida is a closed-primary state — only voters registered with a recognized party can vote in that party's primary. Independent and No-Party-Affiliation voters can vote in nonpartisan primaries (judges, school board, county judge in some races) and on ballot measures, but not in the partisan primaries. The BallotTab disclosure key reads from this field.

races.notes: Open seat — Governor DeSantis is term-limited. Florida runs a closed primary, so only registered Republican / Democratic voters choose between primary candidates on Aug 18. NPA, Libertarian, Constitution, Independent, and Write-in candidates skip the primary and appear on the Nov 3 general ballot.
races.general_candidates_note: Demo projection of the R / D primary winners (update after Aug 18) plus every NPA, minor-party, and write-in candidate who bypassed the primary and goes straight to the Nov 3 ballot.

_roster_source.authority: Florida Department of State, Division of Elections — Candidate Tracking System
_roster_source.note: 48 active filers as of 2026-05-05. List is fluid until the FL qualifying-period close (June 2026).

races.notes: Uthmeier was appointed in 2025 and is seeking a full term. Florida runs a closed primary on Aug 18 — only registered Republican / Democratic voters choose between primary candidates.
races.general_candidates_note: Demo projection of the R / D primary winners (update after Aug 18). No NPA, minor-party, or write-in candidates have filed for AG, so the general ballot is just the two primary winners.

_roster_source.authority: Florida Department of State, Division of Elections — Candidate Tracking System
_roster_source.note: 4 active filers as of 2026-05-05 (2 R, 2 D). List is fluid until the FL qualifying-period close (June 2026).

races.general_candidates_note: Frost is the only Democrat filed and advances to the general unopposed. Montague is the demo projection for the R primary winner — update after Aug 18.
races.notes: Maxwell Frost (D) is the incumbent seeking reelection. The R primary between Montague and White (newcomer) decides who advances. No NPA / minor-party / write-in candidates have filed.

_roster_source.authority: Florida Department of State, Division of Elections — Candidate Tracking System
_roster_source.note: 3 active filers as of 2026-05-05 (1 D incumbent, 2 R challengers). FEC and FL Division of Elections both track US House filers; the FL list is the ballot-access authority for FL congressional races.

races.general_candidates_note: No Democratic, NPA, minor-party, or write-in candidates have filed. Sen. Rodriguez (R, incumbent) is uncontested and advances to the general unopposed.
races.notes: Sen. Ana Maria Rodriguez (R) is the incumbent. As of 2026-05-05 she is the only filer with the FL Division of Elections — no challenger in either party. The qualifying-period close in June may bring additional filers; check back closer to the deadline.

_roster_source.authority: Florida Department of State, Division of Elections — Candidate Tracking System
_roster_source.note: 1 active filer as of 2026-05-05 (Republican incumbent, uncontested).

races.general_candidates_note: Both primaries are uncontested as of 2026-05-05, so Sousa Lazaballet (D) and Jackson (R) project to advance to the Nov 3 general unless another filer enters before the qualifying deadline.
races.notes: Open seat — Rep. Anna Eskamani (D) is not seeking re-election. Felipe Sousa Lazaballet (D) and Bonnie Jackson (R) are the only filers in either primary as of 2026-05-05.

_roster_source.authority: Florida Department of State, Division of Elections — Candidate Tracking System (cross-referenced with Ballotpedia and Wikipedia 2026 FL House election coverage)
_roster_source.note: 2 active filers as of 2026-05-05 (1 D, 1 R). Open seat — Eskamani (D, incumbent) is not running.

state.title: Property-Tax Relief for Homestead Owners Over 65
state.summary: Expands the homestead property-tax exemption for homeowners aged 65 and older by an additional $25,000, bringing the total exemption cap to $75,000 for qualifying residents. Applies to non-school-district millages only.

arguments: Lowers fixed-cost burden on seniors aging in place as insurance and HOA costs climb.
Shifts ~$180M/yr of county tax burden onto working-age homeowners and renters.

state.fiscal_impact: Estimated −$180M/yr in non-school local property-tax revenue statewide by Year 3.

state.title: Right to Clean Water
state.summary: Establishes a fundamental right of Floridians to clean and healthy waters, enforceable against polluters via private action and state Attorney General. Directs the legislature to pass implementing statutes within two years.
state.source: Citizen-initiated (Florida Right to Clean Water Inc.)

arguments: Creates a durable standard surviving administration changes.
Empowers affected residents to act where regulators don't.
Will trigger a wave of litigation against farmers, municipalities, and utilities.

state.fiscal_impact: Indeterminate — enforcement costs depend on implementing statute.

state.title: Independent Redistricting Commission
state.summary: Creates a 12-member citizen redistricting commission to draw congressional and state legislative maps beginning with the 2030 cycle, replacing legislative map-drawing authority.

arguments: Track record in states like California and Michigan shows more competitive districts.
sponsors: League of Women Voters of Florida

arguments: Removes accountability from elected officials to unelected commissioners.
Existing Fair Districts amendments already constrain legislature.

state.fiscal_impact: ~$8M/yr ongoing for commission operations; ~$14M in startup IT and staffing.

miami-dade.title: Affordable Housing Bond — $450M
miami-dade.summary: Authorizes Miami-Dade County to issue up to $450 million in general obligation bonds, funded by a property-tax millage increase of 0.25 mills (~$58/yr for a $300K assessed home), to construct and preserve workforce and senior affordable housing countywide.

arguments: Dedicates 40% to seniors and essential workers.
Adds to property-tax burden during an affordability crisis.
County lacks capacity to deploy funds efficiently.

miami-dade.fiscal_impact: +0.25 mills ad valorem; ~$28M/yr in new debt service over 25 years.

miami-dade.title: Countywide Transit Expansion Surtax Extension
miami-dade.summary: Extends the existing half-cent People's Transportation Tax by 20 years beyond its current 2042 sunset, dedicating 50% of new revenues to the SMART Plan rapid-transit corridors.

orange.title: Rent-Stabilization Advisory Referendum
orange.summary: Non-binding advisory question asking whether Orange County should petition the Florida Legislature to authorize local rent-stabilization ordinances in housing-emergency conditions.
orange.notes: Advisory only — does not create binding policy. A 2022 binding rent-stabilization measure was struck down by Florida courts.

orange.title: Tourist Development Tax Reallocation
orange.summary: Amends the county charter to allow up to 25% of Tourist Development Tax revenues to be directed toward workforce housing and transportation near tourist corridors.

hillsborough.title: Transportation Sales Surtax — 1¢ for 30 Years
hillsborough.summary: Imposes a 1-cent transportation sales surtax for 30 years, with revenues dedicated 45% to road/bridge maintenance, 30% to HART transit, 15% to safety/complete-streets, and 10% to planning.
hillsborough.notes: Successor to the 2018 surtax that was struck down by the Florida Supreme Court over spending-plan language. Drafters say this version cures the earlier defect.

duval.title: JEA Governance Reform
duval.summary: Amends the Jacksonville city charter to require supermajority City Council approval for any sale, merger, or privatization of the JEA public utility.

pinellas.title: Beach Renourishment Surtax
pinellas.summary: Imposes a 0.5% transportation and beach-resilience surtax for 15 years, with 60% dedicated to beach renourishment and coastal-resilience projects and 40% to transportation.

leon.title: Blueprint 2030 Infrastructure Extension
leon.summary: Extends the existing Blueprint infrastructure sales surtax by 10 years beyond its 2039 sunset to fund additional stormwater, parks, and regional mobility projects.

_personalization_hints.notes: Frontend will stitch 'Your Ballot' from: statewide races + statewide measures + county-matched measures + federal race matching user's congressional_district + state legislative races matching user's districts. Municipal races not yet seeded — stubs can be added under races[].jurisdiction once city election dates align.
